items.title: USDA finalizes tougher SNAP retailer stocking standards (7 varieties per category; compliance by Nov. 4, 2026)
items.summary: USDA updated SNAP retailer stocking rules so most SNAP-authorized retailers must offer at least 7 varieties in each of four staple categories (dairy, fruits/vegetables, grains, protein). The rule also requires at least one perishable variety in at least 3 of the 4 categories, with compliance required by Nov. 4, 2026.

items.title: USDA says Montana SNAP waiver to restrict soft drinks, junk food, and candy will take effect in 2026
items.summary: USDA announced it approved a Montana waiver request targeting SNAP purchases of soft drinks, junk food, and candy. USDA said the waiver will go into effect in 2026.

items.title: Nebraska requests USDA approval to restrict candy purchases with SNAP benefits (target start: Nov. 1, 2026)
items.summary: Nebraska DHHS says it submitted a request to USDA to expand the state’s SNAP Healthy Choice Waiver to include candy as an ineligible purchase. If approved, the restriction would take effect Nov. 1, 2026, and Nebraska noted its current waiver already restricts soda and energy drinks (effective Jan. 1, 2026).

items.title: HUD sets Jan. 1, 2027 enforcement date for key HOTMA income/asset changes for most PHAs (work begins Sept. 2026)
items.summary: HUD Notice PIH 2026-15 says HUD will enforce HOTMA sections 102 and 104 starting Jan. 1, 2027 for all public housing agencies except MTW PHAs and PHAs using HUD’s Family Reporting Software (FRS). HUD noted that PHAs starting annual reexams 120 days before the effective date may need to begin using HOTMA policies to collect and verify income beginning in Sept. 2026.
items.source: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development

items.title: Direct Express transitions to a new bank; new enrollments begin May 2026 (existing users transition later in 2026/early 2027)
items.summary: SSA says Treasury selected Fifth Third Bank as the new financial agent for the Direct Express program, and new card enrollments with Fifth Third begin in May 2026. SSA says existing cardholders’ accounts will begin transitioning later in 2026 or early 2027 and advises beneficiaries to keep contact information updated and continue using Comerica-issued cards until they expire.

items.title: SSA OIG audit finds widow(er) benefit underpayments and missed filing-option documentation (estimated $50.4M underpaid)
items.summary: SSA’s Office of Inspector General reported that in its sample review, 59% of widow(er) beneficiaries were paid the correct monthly benefit amount and 41% were paid inappropriately or lacked documentation about filing options. The audit estimated 8,618 widow(er)s were underpaid about $50.4 million, and another 5,367 widow(er)s could have received a projected $113.8 million more by delaying retirement claims until age 70.

items.title: KFF update: 41 states (incl. DC) have adopted Medicaid expansion; 10 states have not
items.summary: KFF reports that Medicaid expansion under the ACA generally covers adults up to 138% of the federal poverty level, which KFF cites as $21,597 for an individual in 2025. As of May 21, 2026, KFF reports 41 states (including DC) have adopted Medicaid expansion and 10 states have not.

items.title: Taxpayer Advocate warns many taxpayers must file by July 10, 2026 to protect potential COVID-era penalty/interest refunds
items.summary: The Taxpayer Advocate Service says relief is not automatic and that most taxpayers who may be entitled to refunds or abatements of COVID-era penalties and interest generally need to file by July 10, 2026 to protect their rights. TAS says taxpayers can use Form 843 (Claim for Refund and Request for Abatement) and may label protective claims (for example, “Protective Refund Claim Pursuant to Kwong Case”).

items.title: USDA publishes WIC income eligibility guidelines for July 1, 2026–June 30, 2027 (example: $29,526 annual for 1 person at 185% level)
items.summary: USDA published updated WIC income eligibility guidelines effective July 1, 2026. For the 48 contiguous states and DC, the reduced-price meal threshold (185% of poverty) is listed as $29,526 annually ($2,461 monthly) for a household of 1 and $61,050 annually ($5,088 monthly) for a household of 4.
